subsystem: Linux kernel BPF (eBPF) subsystem — the map_create() helper that backs the BPF_MAP_CREATE command of the bpf(2) syscall. It validates a userspace-supplied union bpf_attr, allocates a struct bpf_map of the requested type, wires up its BTF type information, security label, IDR id, memcg accounting, and finally installs a file descriptor the caller uses to reference the new map.
provenance: Identified by the unmistakable BPF naming and types: union bpf_attr, struct bpf_map, BPF_MAP_CREATE, BPF_MAP_TYPE_STRUCT_OPS, bpf_map_attr_numa_node, bpf_get_file_flag, find_and_alloc_map, bpf_obj_name_cpy, btf_get_by_fd, map_check_btf, security_bpf_map_alloc, bpf_map_alloc_id, bpf_map_save_memcg, bpf_map_new_fd, bpf_map_put_with_uref. These are all symbols from kernel/bpf/syscall.c in the mainline Linux kernel (GPL-2.0). The sample banner 'primevul_c_train_2894_vuln' and filename 'train_0067_cwe307.c' are dataset metadata, not part of the code; the CWE-307 hint is not credible for this function and is ignored — the code is reviewed on its merits.
trust_boundary: The single parameter 'union bpf_attr *attr' is a copy of a structure supplied directly by an unprivileged (subject to CAP_BPF/CAP_SYS_ADMIN and the unprivileged-bpf sysctl) userspace caller of bpf(2). EVERY field it touches is attacker-controlled: map_type, map_flags, map_name, numa_node, btf_fd, btf_key_type_id, btf_value_type_id, and btf_vmlinux_value_type_id. btf_fd names a userspace-installed BTF object whose blob content is fully attacker-authored. The map object and its callbacks (map->ops->map_free) are kernel-internal, but they are driven entirely by attacker-selected map_type. The hostile inputs are therefore the type-id numbers, the BTF fd/blob, the map_name bytes, and the map_flags/numa_node scalars; the safety of the whole path depends on downstream validators (CHECK_ATTR, bpf_get_file_flag, map_check_btf, bpf_obj_name_cpy) being complete.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point for creating a BPF map. It takes a pointer to a union bpf_attr that the kernel has already copied in from userspace for the BPF_MAP_CREATE command. Locals are declared up front: numa_node is decoded immediately from attr via bpf_map_attr_numa_node(), which extracts the caller-requested NUMA node (and normalizes the 'no preference' case); map will hold the freshly allocated map; f_flags will hold the translated open-file flags; err is the running error code. Because numa_node is derived from attacker input here, its later validation (lines 25-28) is the only guard on it.

CHECK_ATTR is a macro that enforces the syscall ABI contract: it verifies that all bytes of attr outside the field range used by the BPF_MAP_CREATE command are zero. This rejects callers who set fields the kernel version does not understand and prevents stale/garbage bytes from being interpreted. On any mismatch the function returns -EINVAL before allocating anything. This is the first and coarsest input-validation gate.

Consistency checks on the three attacker-supplied BTF type-id selectors. If btf_vmlinux_value_type_id is set (meaning the map value is a kernel vmlinux struct, used by BPF_MAP_TYPE_STRUCT_OPS), then the map_type MUST be STRUCT_OPS and the caller must NOT also provide a btf_key_type_id or an ordinary btf_value_type_id — otherwise -EINVAL. Conversely, if the caller gave a key type id but no value type id, that is nonsensical and rejected. This normalizes the mutually exclusive BTF configurations so later code (lines 45-73) can assume one coherent shape.

attr->map_flags is translated into VFS open-file flags (e.g. read-only / write-only accessibility of the resulting fd) by bpf_get_file_flag(). That helper validates that only permitted flag bits are set and returns a negative errno for unknown/illegal combinations, which is propagated out directly. f_flags is stashed for use when the fd is finally installed at line 85.

Validates the caller-requested NUMA node. If the caller did not request NUMA_NO_NODE, the node index must be in range and currently online. The cast (unsigned int)numa_node is deliberate: it folds any negative value into a large unsigned number so the '>= nr_node_ids' comparison rejects negatives too, avoiding a negative index into per-node arrays. This is a correctly written bounds check on an attacker-controlled index.

The heart of construction: find_and_alloc_map(attr) selects the map_ops implementation for attr->map_type, validates the type-specific size/key/value parameters, and allocates+initializes the struct bpf_map (typically zeroed via kzalloc so fields not touched here are NULL/0). On failure it returns an ERR_PTR, which IS_ERR detects and PTR_ERR converts to an errno returned to the caller. After this point 'map' is a live allocation and every subsequent error must clean it up via the goto labels.

Copies the attacker-supplied map_name bytes into the map's name field. bpf_obj_name_cpy copies at most sizeof(attr->map_name) (BPF_OBJ_NAME_LEN, 16) bytes, is responsible for validating that the name contains only permitted characters and is NUL-terminated, and returns negative on an invalid name. On failure control jumps to free_map to release the just-allocated map. The security of this depends on map->name being at least as large as attr->map_name and on the helper terminating the string.

Initializes the reference-counting and locking state. refcnt (kernel-internal references) and usercnt (userspace-visible references) are both seeded to 1 so the object stays alive through construction. freeze_mutex guards the map-freeze operation. map->spin_lock_off is set to the sentinel -EINVAL meaning 'no bpf_spin_lock field present in the value'; map_check_btf may later overwrite it with the discovered byte offset. This sentinel-initialization pattern is security-relevant: any *_off field consumed elsewhere must be seeded here, or downstream code will treat an uninitialized offset as a real one.

If the caller supplied any BTF type id, the map must be associated with a BTF object. btf_get_by_fd(attr->btf_fd) resolves the attacker-provided file descriptor to a struct btf and takes a reference on it. If the fd is invalid the ERR_PTR is turned into err and control jumps to free_map (at which point map->btf is still NULL, so the cleanup path's btf_put(NULL) is harmless). On success the reference is stored in map->btf, transferring ownership of that reference to the map — from here the free_map path is responsible for dropping it.

When an ordinary btf_value_type_id is present, map_check_btf() walks the attacker-authored BTF blob to resolve the key and value type ids, validate that their sizes match the map's declared key/value sizes, and discover embedded special fields (bpf_spin_lock, and in later kernels bpf_timer) whose byte offsets it records in the map. This is the main untrusted-parsing step in the function. On any error it jumps to free_map, which will drop the btf reference just taken. The three type-id fields are then recorded on the map for later runtime use.

security_bpf_map_alloc attaches an LSM (e.g. SELinux) blob to the map; on failure it jumps to free_map (before any security state exists to free). bpf_map_alloc_id assigns a global integer id and inserts the map into the IDR, which is the point at which the object becomes reachable from OTHER threads via BPF_MAP_GET_FD_BY_ID / BPF_MAP_GET_NEXT_ID even though this thread has not yet returned an fd. Its failure jumps to free_map_sec so the security blob is released first, then falls through to free_map.

bpf_map_save_memcg records the memory cgroup that will be charged for this map's future allocations. bpf_map_new_fd installs an anonymous inode/fd for the map with the previously validated f_flags and returns the fd number (>=0) to userspace, which is the success return value. If fd allocation fails, the object has ALREADY been published by bpf_map_alloc_id, so a plain free would race another thread that grabbed it by id; instead bpf_map_put_with_uref drops both the user reference and the kernel reference so the last putter frees it safely. On success err holds the fd and is returned.

The unwinding ladder. free_map_sec releases the LSM blob (reached only after security_bpf_map_alloc succeeded) and falls through. free_map drops the BTF reference — btf_put(map->btf) is safe when map->btf is NULL, which is the case for every early jump before line 60 — and then invokes the type-specific destructor map->ops->map_free(map) to free the allocation. Note these labels run BEFORE bpf_map_alloc_id has published the object (all gotos are above line 79 except the alloc_id failure which enters at free_map_sec), so a direct free here is not racing the IDR; that is why the post-publish failure at line 85 uses bpf_map_put_with_uref instead of these labels.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the primary untrusted-data surface. attr->btf_fd, attr->btf_key_type_id and attr->btf_value_type_id are all attacker-chosen, and the BTF object they reference contains a fully attacker-authored type graph. map_check_btf() must, using only those inputs, resolve arbitrary type ids, validate that resolved sizes match the map's key/value sizes, and locate special members without ever reading outside the BTF blob. A missing bound on a resolved type id, an unchecked member offset, or trusting a size field lets a crafted BTF blob drive an out-of-bounds read in the verifier or record a bogus spin_lock/timer offset that becomes an OOB write at map runtime. map_create itself does no validation here beyond forwarding — the invariant (every type id is range-checked against the BTF header count and every member offset is bounded by the value size) lives entirely in map_check_btf and the BTF verifier, which are not shown. Latent: the danger is contract-level even though the arithmetic is not in this file.

map->name is filled from the attacker-controlled attr->map_name, bounded by sizeof(attr->map_name) (BPF_OBJ_NAME_LEN = 16). The invariant that must hold in the implementation is that the destination map->name is at least BPF_OBJ_NAME_LEN bytes AND that bpf_obj_name_cpy guarantees NUL termination and rejects non-printable bytes. If map->name were ever declared smaller than the source bound, or if the helper copied the full length without reserving a terminator, this is a classic fixed-size buffer copy that overflows or produces an unterminated string later formatted with %s (info leak). The copy uses the SOURCE size as the bound, which is the correct idiom only when destination >= source; confirm that relationship in the struct definition.

bpf_map_alloc_id() publishes the map into the global IDR at line 79, but bpf_map_save_memcg() does not run until line 83. Between those two points the map is reachable by a concurrent thread through BPF_MAP_GET_FD_BY_ID, yet its memcg accounting pointer is not yet set. If any operation reachable via the id path dereferences the memcg/objcg association before line 83 completes, that is a transient NULL/uninitialized-state window (a publish-before-fully-initialized race). The authors correctly recognize the publish semantics for the fd-failure case — the comment at 87-92 explains why bpf_map_put_with_uref is required instead of a plain free — but the same publish window governs save_memcg ordering. Invariant to confirm: all per-map state that an id-lookup consumer may read must be initialized before bpf_map_alloc_id, or the consumers must tolerate the not-yet-set state.

The cleanup drops the BTF reference and then calls the type destructor. The safety of btf_put(map->btf) on the early paths depends on map->btf having been zero-initialized by find_and_alloc_map (kzalloc) so that pre-line-60 jumps put NULL, and on ownership being transferred (not shared) at line 60 so that map->ops->map_free does not ALSO drop the same btf reference — otherwise this is a double put (reference underflow) leading to a premature free / use-after-free of the BTF object. Equally, map->ops->map_free(map) runs on a map that may be only partially constructed (e.g. jump from line 38 before btf/security/id exist); each type's map_free must tolerate a half-initialized map. Invariant: map->btf is NULL until line 60, ownership is exclusive to the btf_put here, and every map_free is safe on a partially initialized object.

Only spin_lock_off is seeded to the 'absent' sentinel here. This function predates or omits the initialization of any sibling special-field offset (e.g. map->timer_off in later kernels). Any *_off field that map_check_btf may set but that is NOT seeded to a sentinel before use will contain whatever find_and_alloc_map left there; if that value is treated as a valid byte offset into the map value at runtime, it becomes an out-of-bounds read/write primitive on the map element. This is an uninitialized-state hazard: the invariant is that EVERY special-field offset consumed by helpers such as bpf_spin_lock/bpf_timer is explicitly initialized to its 'not present' sentinel here before map_check_btf can conditionally overwrite it.

CHECK_ATTR is the only guarantee that fields outside the BPF_MAP_CREATE window are zero. If the macro's field range is not kept in sync when a new attr field is added to this command, a newly consumed field could carry stale non-zero bytes from userspace that bypass the zero-check, or conversely a legitimately-set new field could be rejected. This is a maintenance/ABI hazard rather than a memory-safety bug in the shown lines, but it is the backstop the rest of the function trusts; confirm the CHECK_ATTR field list covers every attr member this function reads (map_type, map_flags, map_name, numa_node, btf_fd, btf_*_type_id).

summary: This is the mainline Linux BPF map_create() path; the whole attack surface is the attacker-controlled union bpf_attr driving map allocation and, above all, BTF processing. The function's own validation (CHECK_ATTR, flag/numa checks, mutually-exclusive BTF-id checks) is sound and its error-unwinding and post-publish put_with_uref handling look correct, so there is no self-contained memory-safety bug visible in these lines. The real risk is latent and contract-level: the untrusted-BTF parse in map_check_btf (OOB/validation), the destination-size and termination contract of bpf_obj_name_cpy, exclusive ownership of the single map->btf reference across the cleanup path, and complete sentinel-initialization of every special-field offset (spin_lock_off is seeded; confirm no sibling *_off is left uninitialized).

reviewed_anchors.reason: Sole parameter is a fully attacker-controlled userspace structure; establishes the trust boundary for every field read below.
Result is bounds-checked at lines 25-28 with a correct unsigned cast; no unchecked index reaches an array here.
Only guard that out-of-command attr bytes are zero; an ABI/field-range mismatch would let stale attacker bytes through — maintenance hazard worth confirming.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: attr->btf_vmlinux_value_type_id / map_type != BPF_MAP_TYPE_STRUCT_OPS / btf_key_type_id / btf_value_type_id checks (lines 13-19)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Correct mutually-exclusive validation of the BTF-id selectors; rejects incoherent combinations with -EINVAL.
Validates flag bits and returns negative errno on illegal combinations, which is propagated; no unchecked flag reaches the fd.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: numa_node range/online check with (unsigned int) cast (lines 25-28)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Correctly folds negatives via the unsigned cast and checks nr_node_ids and node_online; a properly written bounds check.
Return checked with IS_ERR/PTR_ERR; allocation and type-specific validation live in the callee, not in the shown contract-violating way.
Attacker-controlled name copied into a fixed field; safety depends on dest size >= source and guaranteed NUL termination — latent fixed-buffer contract.
Standard refcount/lock initialization; no attacker input and no size/offset contract.
Sentinel-init of a special-field offset; any sibling *_off field left uninitialized becomes an OOB primitive at map runtime — uninitialized-state hazard.
Resolves an attacker fd to a BTF object and takes a reference stored in map->btf; ownership must be exclusive to the cleanup btf_put.
Main untrusted-BTF parse; unchecked type ids/member offsets in the callee are the central OOB surface reached from this command.
Plain record of already-validated type ids onto the map; no size arithmetic here.
Failure jumps to free_map before any security state exists; ordering is correct.
Publishes the map to the global IDR (userspace-reachable) before memcg is saved and before an fd exists — creates the publish window / dictates the put_with_uref cleanup.
Runs AFTER bpf_map_alloc_id, so an id-lookup consumer could observe the map before memcg accounting is set — ordering/race to confirm.
Post-publish fd-failure is correctly handled with put_with_uref (drops user+kernel refs) rather than a racy direct free; the comment documents the reasoning.
Reached only after security_bpf_map_alloc succeeded; correct fall-through into free_map.
Drops the single BTF reference; correctness depends on map->btf being NULL on early paths and ownership not being duplicated by map_free — double-put / UAF risk if violated.
Type destructor; must tolerate a partially initialized map, but that is a callee contract with no attacker-controlled size in the shown line.

findings.title: Untrusted BTF parsing driven by attacker fd and type ids (map_check_btf)
findings.anchor: btf = btf_get_by_fd(attr->btf_fd); ... map_check_btf(map, btf, attr->btf_key_type_id, attr->btf_value_type_id)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read / missing input validation in untrusted type-metadata parse
findings.invariant: map_check_btf and the underlying BTF verifier must range-check every type id against the BTF header's type count and bound every resolved member offset by the map's value size before recording spin_lock/timer offsets or accepting the value type.
findings.failure_mode: A caller passes a valid btf_fd whose blob encodes a crafted type graph plus attacker-chosen btf_key_type_id/btf_value_type_id. If the verifier trusts an out-of-range type id or an unchecked member offset, it reads outside the BTF blob (info leak / crash) or records a bogus special-field offset that becomes an out-of-bounds write when the map element is later accessed by a bpf_spin_lock/bpf_timer helper.
findings.what_to_confirm: That map_check_btf validates btf_key_type_id/btf_value_type_id against btf->nr_types and bounds every discovered member offset (spin_lock_off/timer_off) within the value size.

findings.title: map->name filled from attacker map_name — destination size and NUL-termination contract
findings.bug_class: Fixed-size buffer copy without independent destination bound
findings.invariant: map->name must be at least sizeof(attr->map_name) (BPF_OBJ_NAME_LEN, 16) bytes and bpf_obj_name_cpy must guarantee NUL termination and reject non-printable/embedded-NUL names.
findings.failure_mode: The copy is bounded by the SOURCE size, which is only safe if the destination is at least as large. If map->name is smaller, or if the helper copies the full 16 bytes without reserving a terminator, an unterminated map->name later formatted with %s leaks adjacent kernel memory, or a mismatched destination overflows.
findings.what_to_confirm: The declared size of struct bpf_map::name versus BPF_OBJ_NAME_LEN, and that bpf_obj_name_cpy always NUL-terminates and validates the character set.

findings.title: Single map->btf reference must be exclusively owned by the free_map cleanup
findings.bug_class: Reference-count underflow / use-after-free on BTF object
findings.invariant: map->btf is NULL on every error path taken before line 60 (guaranteed by find_and_alloc_map zeroing the map), and the reference stored at line 60 is dropped exactly once — only by this btf_put — with no map->ops->map_free implementation also releasing it.
findings.failure_mode: If any map type's map_free also calls btf_put on map->btf, or if map->btf is left non-NULL from a stale allocation, the reference is dropped twice, freeing the BTF object while another map/prog still references it — a use-after-free. Conversely a missed put on some path leaks the BTF reference.
findings.what_to_confirm: That struct bpf_map is kzalloc'd (map->btf starts NULL) and that no map_ops::map_free callback drops map->btf, so this btf_put is the sole owner-release.

findings.title: Map published to IDR before memcg accounting and fd install (publish window)
findings.invariant: All per-map state that a concurrent BPF_MAP_GET_FD_BY_ID consumer may dereference (including memcg/objcg association) must either be initialized before bpf_map_alloc_id publishes the map, or every such consumer must tolerate the not-yet-set state.
findings.failure_mode: After line 79 the map is reachable by another thread via its id, but bpf_map_save_memcg only runs at line 83. A concurrent operation that assumes memcg accounting is set could observe a NULL/uninitialized association in that window. The authors handle the fd-failure publish correctly with bpf_map_put_with_uref, but the save_memcg ordering leaves a transient inconsistency.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether any id-reachable code path dereferences the memcg/objcg pointer, and whether save_memcg should precede bpf_map_alloc_id.

findings.title: Only spin_lock_off is sentinel-initialized; sibling special-field offsets may be uninitialized
findings.bug_class: Use of uninitialized special-field offset as a byte index
findings.invariant: Every special-field offset that map_check_btf may conditionally set and that a runtime helper later treats as a byte offset into the map value must be explicitly seeded to its 'not present' sentinel here before that parse runs.
findings.failure_mode: If a sibling offset field (e.g. timer_off in later kernels) is consumed by a helper but is not seeded to a sentinel, it holds whatever find_and_alloc_map left there; a non-sentinel garbage value is treated as a valid offset and used as an out-of-bounds read/write index into a map element.
findings.what_to_confirm: The full set of *_off / special-field offset members on struct bpf_map and that each is initialized to its sentinel before map_check_btf runs.

audit_checklist: map_check_btf: confirm btf_key_type_id/btf_value_type_id are range-checked against the BTF type count and that every discovered member offset is bounded by the map value size before being recorded.
struct bpf_map::name size vs BPF_OBJ_NAME_LEN, and that bpf_obj_name_cpy always NUL-terminates and validates the character set.
Confirm struct bpf_map is kzalloc'd so map->btf is NULL on all pre-line-60 error paths, making btf_put(map->btf) safe.
Confirm no map_ops::map_free callback also drops map->btf (avoid double btf_put / UAF).
Enumerate every special-field offset on struct bpf_map (spin_lock_off, timer_off, etc.) and verify each is sentinel-initialized before map_check_btf can set it.
Determine whether any BPF_MAP_GET_FD_BY_ID-reachable path dereferences memcg state, and whether bpf_map_save_memcg should run before bpf_map_alloc_id.
Verify CHECK_ATTR(BPF_MAP_CREATE) field range covers every attr member read here (map_type, map_flags, map_name, numa_node, btf_fd, btf_*_type_id).
Confirm each map type's map_free tolerates a partially constructed map (early goto from line 38 before btf/security/id are set up).
